title: ECG — Approach (Rate/Rhythm/Axis)
oneLiner: Systematic ECG interpretation (rate/rhythm/axis) to rapidly identify life-threatening arrhythmias, ischaemia, and conduction blocks in unwell patients.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia / Ventricular Fibrillation**  
  Single finding: No palpable pulse + wide QRS >120ms or chaotic base-line → **immediate defibrillation** (200J biphasic) before anything else.
- **Complete Heart Block (Third-degree AV block)**  
  Single finding: Ventricular rate <40 bpm + P-P constant but no relation to QRS → **standby transcutaneous pacing**; do not wait for senior review if patient is hypotensive.
- **Massive ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)**  
  Single finding: >1mm STE in ≥2 contiguous leads (especially anterior/V1-V4) + chest pain → **activate primary PCI pathway** immediately.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms prompting ECG:**  
  – Chest pain (pressure, burning, pleuritic)  
  – Syncope / near-syncope  
  – Palpitations (fluttering, skipped beats)  
  – Unexplained SOB, dizziness, or collapse  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  – Pulse irregularity (fast/slow/irregular)  
  – Hypotension (SBP <90) or signs of shock  
  – JVP raised (especially with bradycardia → think heart block)  
  – Signs of heart failure (crackles, peripheral oedema)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Wide complex tachycardia** (QRS >120ms):  
  – Ventricular tachycardia (VT) – most likely if patient >35yrs or known heart disease  
  – SVT with aberrancy – history of bundle branch block or pre-excitation  
  – Hyperkalaemia – broad QRS, tall T waves, no pulse usually  
- **Narrow complex tachycardia** (QRS <120ms):  
  – Atrial fibrillation (AF) – irregularly irregular, no P waves  
  – Atrial flutter – “sawtooth” pattern, regular ventricular response  
  – SVT (AVNRT/AVRT) – regular narrow-complex, sudden onset/offset  
- **Bradycardia** (rate <60):  
  – Sinus bradycardia – gradual, P waves present  
  – AV block (1st, 2nd, 3rd) – check PR interval and QRS-p wave relationship  
  – Sick sinus syndrome – pauses >3s, often with tachy-brady pattern  
- **Axis deviation:**  
  – Left axis (QRS positive in I, negative in aVF) – may be normal, LBBB, left anterior fascicular block  
  – Right axis (negative in I, positive in aVF) – RVH, PE, lateral MI  
- **Splitter:**  
  For wide-complex tachycardia: **Fusion beats or capture beats** → ventricular tachycardia; absent → consider SVT with aberrancy.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  – 12-lead ECG (immediate)  
  – Continuous cardiac monitoring (defibrillator pads on)  
  – Manual pulse check if pulseless (do not rely on monitor artefact)  
- **Bloods:**  
  – Full blood count, U&Es, Mg, Ca (electrolytes can cause arrhythmias)  
  – Troponin (high-sensitivity) – draw on arrival and at 3h  
  – BNP if heart failure suspected  
- **Imaging:**  
  – Chest X-ray (look for cardiomegaly, pulmonary oedema, widened mediastinum)  
  – Echocardiogram (urgent if haemodynamically unstable)  
- **Special tests:**  
  – Adenosine diagnostic – if wide complex tachycardia is uncertain (narrow alternative SVT)  
  – CT pulmonary angiogram (if PE suspected as cause of right axis/wide QRS)  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**  
  – **Wellens’ criteria** (for LAD/right axis in anterior STEMI – leads V2-V5)  
  – **Sgarbossa criteria** (for MI in LBBB – concordant STE >1mm)  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  **12-lead ECG in any patient with syncope, chest pain, or palpitations** – omitting this is a serious error (misses potential STEMI, arrhythmia, or heart block).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – **Pulseless VT/VF:** Defibrillate 200J biphasic → CPR → shock every 2min + adrenaline 1mg IV q3-5min after 3rd shock.  
  – **Stable VT (with pulse):** Amiodarone 300mg IV over 10-20min (or synchronised cardioversion if unstable).  
  – **Unstable bradycardia (SBP<90 / shock):** Atropine 500mcg IV (repeat up to 3mg) then transcutaneous pacing if no response.  
  – **AF with rapid ventricular rate:** Rate control – bisoprolol 2.5-5mg PO or IV (or verapamil 5mg IV if no heart failure).  
  – **STEMI:** Activate primary PCI team immediately; give aspirin 300mg PO (chewed) + ticagrelor 180mg PO (if no contraindications).  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  – Telemetry monitoring (for any new arrhythmia)  
  – Correct electrolytes (K+ >4.0, Mg >0.8)  
  – Treat underlying cause (ischaemia, sepsis, electrolyte imbalance)  
  – For new AF: anticoagulation assessment (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Outpatient echocardiogram for structural heart disease  
  – Holter monitor if palpitations but normal ECG  
  – ICD for survivors of VT/VF (after reversible causes excluded)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – Fluids: 0.9% saline – 500mL IV over 2h if hypotensive, but avoid overload in heart failure  
  – VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (adjust for renal)  
  – Diet: NBM if unstable or possible endoscopy needed  
  – Observations: 4-hourly (hourly if unstable) + continuous cardiac monitor
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Adenosine:** 6mg IV rapid bolus (then 12mg if no effect) – for SVT; **CI:** asthma (can cause bronchospasm), 2nd/3rd degree block  
- **Amiodarone:** 300mg IV in 10-20min (then 900mg/24h via central line) – for VT/AF; **CI:** thyroid disease, pulmonary fibrosis, bradycardia  
- **Atropine:** 500mcg IV q3-5min (max 3mg) – for bradycardia; **CI:** glaucoma, myasthenia gravis  
- **Bisoprolol:** 1.25-5mg PO once daily – for rate control in AF; **CI:** heart block, asthma, severe COPD  
- **Verapamil:** 5mg IV over 2min (repeat after 10min) – for SVT/AF; **CI:** heart failure, concurrent beta-blocker, hypotension  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  – **Amiodarone:** Rapid infusion can cause hypotension; monitor for phlebitis – always use central line if >1 hour infusion.  
  – **Atropine:** Doses <0.5mg can cause paradoxical bradycardia – use 500mcg as minimum.  
  – **Verapamil:** Never give to wide complex tachycardia of uncertain origin (may cause VF).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  – Enoxaparin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min.  
  – Adenosine: no dose change but use caution in advanced CKD.  
  – Amiodarone: no dose change in renal, but hepatic impairment – reduce or avoid.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  – Hypotension from arrhythmia (especially VT, AF with rapid rate)  
  – Cardiac arrest (VT/VF → asystole)  
  – Myocardial ischaemia/infarction (from tachyarrhythmia or severe bradycardia)  
- **Chronic:**  
  – Atrial fibrillation → stroke (if no anticoagulation)  
  – Heart failure secondary to tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy  
  – ICD/pacemaker infection or lead issues
sections.Prognosis:
- **VT/VF:** Good if defibrillated within 3-5 minutes (survival >70%); poor if delayed.  
- **AF:** 1.5-2x increased stroke risk (with CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2); manageable with rate/rhythm control + anticoagulation.  
- **Complete heart block:** Permanent pacing required; recovery depends on cause (ischaemia often reverses if reperfused).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always measure rate + QRS duration first** – this immediately classifies arrhythmia as fast/slow and narrow/wide.  
- **Use the 12-lead systematically:** Rate → Rhythm → Axis → Intervals (PR, QRS, QT) → ST segment → T waves.  
- **If bradycardic but not acutely sick, get a 12-lead and check previous ECGs** – may be athlete’s heart or rate-controlling drugs.  
- **For wide complex tachycardia, always assume VT until proven otherwise** (especially in elderly or heart disease) – treat accordingly; avoid verapamil.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Treating artefact as VF** – always check the patient’s pulse before shocking; if unsure, stop briefly and look at leads.  
- **Ignoring subtle ST elevation in posterior/lateral leads** – add V7-V9 or right-sided leads if suspect posterior MI (e.g., ST depression in V1-V3 with upright T waves).  
- **Giving verapamil in wide complex tachycardia** – can cause severe hypotension and VF; never use unless you are confident it’s SVT with aberrancy.  
- **Forgetting to check K+ before treating arrhythmia** – hyperkalaemia can mimic VT with wide QRS; treat with calcium gluconate first.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  – New chest pain, syncope, or sustained palpitations  
  – SOB, dizziness, or altered consciousness  
  – Any episode of collapse or sudden fall  
- **Follow-up:**  
  – Cardiology clinic within 2-4 weeks (urgent if recent arrhythmia)  
  – Holter monitor arranged if paroxysmal symptoms but normal ECG  
  – Anticoagulation clinic for AF if newly started (INR or DOAC monitoring)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/Crash team if:**  
  – NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially respiratory rate >24 or SpO2 <92%)  
  – Lactate > 2 mmol/L (rising)  
  – GCS ≤ 14 (drop from baseline)  
  – New arrhythmia on monitor with hypotension (SBP <90) or chest pain  
  – Failure of first-line treatment (e.g., no response to two shocks for VT/VF)  
  – Any pulseless rhythm → **activated crash call immediately**  
- **Escalate to ICU if:**  
  – Persistent arrhythmia despite medical treatment  
  – Need for inotropic support (e.g., noradrenaline)  
  – Post-cardiac arrest with ROSC but unconscious

rawMarkdown:
## ECG — Approach (Rate/Rhythm/Axis)
**One-liner:** Systematic ECG interpretation (rate/rhythm/axis) to rapidly identify life-threatening arrhythmias, ischaemia, and conduction blocks in unwell patients.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia / Ventricular Fibrillation**  
  Single finding: No palpable pulse + wide QRS >120ms or chaotic base-line → **immediate defibrillation** (200J biphasic) before anything else.
- **Complete Heart Block (Third-degree AV block)**  
  Single finding: Ventricular rate <40 bpm + P-P constant but no relation to QRS → **standby transcutaneous pacing**; do not wait for senior review if patient is hypotensive.
- **Massive ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)**  
  Single finding: >1mm STE in ≥2 contiguous leads (especially anterior/V1-V4) + chest pain → **activate primary PCI pathway** immediately.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms prompting ECG:**  
  – Chest pain (pressure, burning, pleuritic)  
  – Syncope / near-syncope  
  – Palpitations (fluttering, skipped beats)  
  – Unexplained SOB, dizziness, or collapse  
- **Key exam findings:**  
  – Pulse irregularity (fast/slow/irregular)  
  – Hypotension (SBP <90) or signs of shock  
  – JVP raised (especially with bradycardia → think heart block)  
  – Signs of heart failure (crackles, peripheral oedema)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Wide complex tachycardia** (QRS >120ms):  
  – Ventricular tachycardia (VT) – most likely if patient >35yrs or known heart disease  
  – SVT with aberrancy – history of bundle branch block or pre-excitation  
  – Hyperkalaemia – broad QRS, tall T waves, no pulse usually  
- **Narrow complex tachycardia** (QRS <120ms):  
  – Atrial fibrillation (AF) – irregularly irregular, no P waves  
  – Atrial flutter – “sawtooth” pattern, regular ventricular response  
  – SVT (AVNRT/AVRT) – regular narrow-complex, sudden onset/offset  
- **Bradycardia** (rate <60):  
  – Sinus bradycardia – gradual, P waves present  
  – AV block (1st, 2nd, 3rd) – check PR interval and QRS-p wave relationship  
  – Sick sinus syndrome – pauses >3s, often with tachy-brady pattern  
- **Axis deviation:**  
  – Left axis (QRS positive in I, negative in aVF) – may be normal, LBBB, left anterior fascicular block  
  – Right axis (negative in I, positive in aVF) – RVH, PE, lateral MI  
- **Splitter:**  
  For wide-complex tachycardia: **Fusion beats or capture beats** → ventricular tachycardia; absent → consider SVT with aberrancy.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  – 12-lead ECG (immediate)  
  – Continuous cardiac monitoring (defibrillator pads on)  
  – Manual pulse check if pulseless (do not rely on monitor artefact)  
- **Bloods:**  
  – Full blood count, U&Es, Mg, Ca (electrolytes can cause arrhythmias)  
  – Troponin (high-sensitivity) – draw on arrival and at 3h  
  – BNP if heart failure suspected  
- **Imaging:**  
  – Chest X-ray (look for cardiomegaly, pulmonary oedema, widened mediastinum)  
  – Echocardiogram (urgent if haemodynamically unstable)  
- **Special tests:**  
  – Adenosine diagnostic – if wide complex tachycardia is uncertain (narrow alternative SVT)  
  – CT pulmonary angiogram (if PE suspected as cause of right axis/wide QRS)  
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**  
  – **Wellens’ criteria** (for LAD/right axis in anterior STEMI – leads V2-V5)  
  – **Sgarbossa criteria** (for MI in LBBB – concordant STE >1mm)  
- **Never-miss test:**  
  **12-lead ECG in any patient with syncope, chest pain, or palpitations** – omitting this is a serious error (misses potential STEMI, arrhythmia, or heart block).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – **Pulseless VT/VF:** Defibrillate 200J biphasic → CPR → shock every 2min + adrenaline 1mg IV q3-5min after 3rd shock.  
  – **Stable VT (with pulse):** Amiodarone 300mg IV over 10-20min (or synchronised cardioversion if unstable).  
  – **Unstable bradycardia (SBP<90 / shock):** Atropine 500mcg IV (repeat up to 3mg) then transcutaneous pacing if no response.  
  – **AF with rapid ventricular rate:** Rate control – bisoprolol 2.5-5mg PO or IV (or verapamil 5mg IV if no heart failure).  
  – **STEMI:** Activate primary PCI team immediately; give aspirin 300mg PO (chewed) + ticagrelor 180mg PO (if no contraindications).  
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  – Telemetry monitoring (for any new arrhythmia)  
  – Correct electrolytes (K+ >4.0, Mg >0.8)  
  – Treat underlying cause (ischaemia, sepsis, electrolyte imbalance)  
  – For new AF: anticoagulation assessment (CHA₂DS₂-VASc)  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Outpatient echocardiogram for structural heart disease  
  – Holter monitor if palpitations but normal ECG  
  – ICD for survivors of VT/VF (after reversible causes excluded)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – Fluids: 0.9% saline – 500mL IV over 2h if hypotensive, but avoid overload in heart failure  
  – VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (adjust for renal)  
  – Diet: NBM if unstable or possible endoscopy needed  
  – Observations: 4-hourly (hourly if unstable) + continuous cardiac monitor  

### Drugs & Doses
- **Adenosine:** 6mg IV rapid bolus (then 12mg if no effect) – for SVT; **CI:** asthma (can cause bronchospasm), 2nd/3rd degree block  
- **Amiodarone:** 300mg IV in 10-20min (then 900mg/24h via central line) – for VT/AF; **CI:** thyroid disease, pulmonary fibrosis, bradycardia  
- **Atropine:** 500mcg IV q3-5min (max 3mg) – for bradycardia; **CI:** glaucoma, myasthenia gravis  
- **Bisoprolol:** 1.25-5mg PO once daily – for rate control in AF; **CI:** heart block, asthma, severe COPD  
- **Verapamil:** 5mg IV over 2min (repeat after 10min) – for SVT/AF; **CI:** heart failure, concurrent beta-blocker, hypotension  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  – **Amiodarone:** Rapid infusion can cause hypotension; monitor for phlebitis – always use central line if >1 hour infusion.  
  – **Atropine:** Doses <0.5mg can cause paradoxical bradycardia – use 500mcg as minimum.  
  – **Verapamil:** Never give to wide complex tachycardia of uncertain origin (may cause VF).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  – Enoxaparin: reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min.  
  – Adenosine: no dose change but use caution in advanced CKD.  
  – Amiodarone: no dose change in renal, but hepatic impairment – reduce or avoid.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  – Hypotension from arrhythmia (especially VT, AF with rapid rate)  
  – Cardiac arrest (VT/VF → asystole)  
  – Myocardial ischaemia/infarction (from tachyarrhythmia or severe bradycardia)  
- **Chronic:**  
  – Atrial fibrillation → stroke (if no anticoagulation)  
  – Heart failure secondary to tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy  
  – ICD/pacemaker infection or lead issues

### Prognosis
- **VT/VF:** Good if defibrillated within 3-5 minutes (survival >70%); poor if delayed.  
- **AF:** 1.5-2x increased stroke risk (with CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2); manageable with rate/rhythm control + anticoagulation.  
- **Complete heart block:** Permanent pacing required; recovery depends on cause (ischaemia often reverses if reperfused).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always measure rate + QRS duration first** – this immediately classifies arrhythmia as fast/slow and narrow/wide.  
- **Use the 12-lead systematically:** Rate → Rhythm → Axis → Intervals (PR, QRS, QT) → ST segment → T waves.  
- **If bradycardic but not acutely sick, get a 12-lead and check previous ECGs** – may be athlete’s heart or rate-controlling drugs.  
- **For wide complex tachycardia, always assume VT until proven otherwise** (especially in elderly or heart disease) – treat accordingly; avoid verapamil.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Treating artefact as VF** – always check the patient’s pulse before shocking; if unsure, stop briefly and look at leads.  
- **Ignoring subtle ST elevation in posterior/lateral leads** – add V7-V9 or right-sided leads if suspect posterior MI (e.g., ST depression in V1-V3 with upright T waves).  
- **Giving verapamil in wide complex tachycardia** – can cause severe hypotension and VF; never use unless you are confident it’s SVT with aberrancy.  
- **Forgetting to check K+ before treating arrhythmia** – hyperkalaemia can mimic VT with wide QRS; treat with calcium gluconate first.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  – New chest pain, syncope, or sustained palpitations  
  – SOB, dizziness, or altered consciousness  
  – Any episode of collapse or sudden fall  
- **Follow-up:**  
  – Cardiology clinic within 2-4 weeks (urgent if recent arrhythmia)  
  – Holter monitor arranged if paroxysmal symptoms but normal ECG  
  – Anticoagulation clinic for AF if newly started (INR or DOAC monitoring)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/Crash team if:**  
  – NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially respiratory rate >24 or SpO2 <92%)  
  – Lactate > 2 mmol/L (rising)  
  – GCS ≤ 14 (drop from baseline)  
  – New arrhythmia on monitor with hypotension (SBP <90) or chest pain  
  – Failure of first-line treatment (e.g., no response to two shocks for VT/VF)  
  – Any pulseless rhythm → **activated crash call immediately**  
- **Escalate to ICU if:**  
  – Persistent arrhythmia despite medical treatment  
  – Need for inotropic support (e.g., noradrenaline)  
  – Post-cardiac arrest with ROSC but unconscious
